text: I love going back and looking at this stuff, and I’ve been thinking a lot about what a modern text-focused interface would be like. It feels like there’s a bunch of neat stuff we should be able to do now that just wouldn’t have been feasible at the time. It’s especially interesting to imagine using something like GPT-3 to get the interface to react to you much more naturally than it would otherwise.

text: For me, the first thing I always do is say thank you. People almost never have to give you feedback, and it's almost always helpful. After that, if I feel it's appropriate, I try to give a thoughtful response. I find it's difficult to do this without sounding defensive though, so that's a particular area I wonder if there's a technique or mental model that would help to not get your emotions involved.

text:
oh boy, I feel like there are SO many factors here. Here’s all things I think about :

• *First filter, should I even process this?* What medium / context was it given through? Social media feedback I’ve found to be anecdotally very useless. A long Github comment with helpful context or suggestions is better. Is the feedback personal / a personal attack? I almost immediately walk away!
• *Assuming it made it past the quality filter..* I think about their context and my context. If someone is complaining about an issue but doesn’t meet my targeted personas I’m supporting (even though we dream of supporting the amorphous ‘everybody’, there’s always some optimized set of personas IMO) then I try to factor that in. It could be that their feedback is _reasonable, but not relevant to my own goals for the project._
• *What’s the situational context.* Are they a customer / potential customer or an OSS contributor, a user, or just a critic? For the first one I agree with being very nice and thankful and friendly and maybe seeking more clarity.
• *What’s the underlying / hidden truth or kernel in what they’re saying?* This is something product managers know well. People say things like “I want to do X feature on your site locally” but sometimes they really just want the speed of the feature to be FAST on your website. So you may have to dig deeper.

text: <@UEBG0NPDK> Not on Lifestreams, but rather on a similar system in the context of a startup a long time ago. That was what led me to write the first purpose-built Semantic Web-style Triplestore. The sort of "global personal assistant" was scraping data from email, contacts, appointments, browsing, &c, and storing everything it learnt in a per user TripleStore to allow inferences, lookup, and so on. There was a temporal aspect to the data which allowed one to make "what was that website I visited about topic X within the last week?" sort of queries.

text:
The design education process is one of the best I've encountered for actively fostering a culture of giving and receiving a good critique. Here's the slightest gloss of the approach:
<https://www.aiga.org/how-to-give-receive-design-critique>
... but you can find much more if you google "design education receiving criticism".

attachments.title: How to give and receive a good design critique
attachments.text: As designers, we don’t design in a vacuum. Here are some quick tips on how to give–and receive–good design feedback during a critique.

text: One reason these things are salient for me is that my computing path was unusual: I went from little 8-bit micros directly to Unix machines (first terminals, then workstations) in the mid-80s. Consequently, my formative history with GUIs included oddities like NeWS, X, Plan9, NeXTSTEP, and a couple kinds of Lisp Machine, but mostly did not include Macs or PCs.

text: I think <@UAJKEBGP8> brings up a great point: there's a distinction to be made here between _negative_ feedback, and _critical_ feedback. Purely negative feedback ("this is crap", "why would you waste your time on that?") usually comes from people who wish you ill, but the best critical feedback comes from those who want you to succeed, and who are well-intended in their criticisms (that's not to say that this sort of feedback can't be incisive -- it often is, but i'll deal with that later). In my view, the only feedback that you should consider acting on is that which comes from the sorts of people who mean well. But just like hearing your mother shouting "clean your room!", it is often difficult and uncomfortable to deal with such advice, and it often comes across as curt or abrasive. The best way I've found is to look at feedback, not as a criticism of my "self", but rather as being critical of some work I've produced or some action I've taken. That may sound cliché, but if someone's well-intentioned criticism of your work bothers you deeply, you need to examine your personal reasons for why that is the case. Like Marcus Aurelius said: _“we all love ourselves more than other people, but care more about their opinion than our own”_ :deciduous_tree:

text:
> if someone's well-intentioned criticism of your work bothers you deeply, you need to examine your personal reasons for why that is the case.
If you've worked hard on something, and you're invested in it, the expectation is that criticism would bother you, is it not? Well-intentioned most of all, I'd imagine, because then it's more likely to be true? That's sort of what I'm looking for, what's the mental model you'd use to remove emotion from the equation?

Examples would take too much context unfortunately, but generally, I'm talking about good feedback. And I guess I would say I usually respond fine. Re Aurelius, I'm actually pretty internally motivated. But something still always bothers me, these interactions *never feel graceful*. I always feel like I gave something up when I respond if that makes any sense. So I was wondering how others handle it and think about it.

attachments.fallback:
[August 28th, 2020 10:10 AM] kalavox: What started with me reverse engineering notion became a data-first recursive UI resolver I called *<https://github.com/den1k/root|root>*.

Here's how it differs from most common technologies today:
• Approaches to UI development like react.js + graphQL require UI components to request data in a shape that satisfies the UI tree. This means the shape of the data is determined by the UI tree. Root takes an inverse approach where the UI tree is determined by the shape of the data.
• A major benefit of this approach is that the UI layout is thus entirely determined by data, data that can be traversed, transformed and stored in arbitrary ways and by arbitrary means.
• This is powerful for unstructured, user-determined, block-based UI's like rich documents (think Roam Research, Notion etc.) enabling queries and functions that, based on users' demands, derive the optimal presentation of a document.
It packs a few more punches. The best example is probably this <https://den1k.github.io/root/rich-document.html|notion-like demo> in about 200 LoC (<https://github.com/den1k/root/blob/master/dev/examples/rich_document/views.cljs|source>). Github repo <https://github.com/den1k/root|here>.

text:
Advice from people who are sincere toward you is not more or less liable to be objectively true, it’s just likely to be more carefully considered and deliberate than feedback that comes from someone whose intentions are less-than-sincere.

When it comes to responding to feedback with grace, that’s definitely an art, and one that I need to practice on a daily basis. In addition to putting myself in the feedback-giver’s shoes, it’s helpful to do things to moderate my reactions in the moment: “pressing the pause button”, so to speak, is super underrated. Whenever possible, I try to wait a length of time that’s proportional to the intensity of my initial reaction to a criticism I get before I respond to it. In cases where I’m very firmly invested in the work that I’ve done, this might be days, but in cases like this (where I’ve received good feedback, and the downside to sharing my reaction is fairly limited), a couple minutes is often enough. I find this most challenging to do in face-to-face settings, which is the reason I tend to solicit feedback primarily in written form (which has mercifully become more acceptable as a result of _certain world events_). If I don’t think I can respond to a given piece of feedback in a manner that I would appreciate coming from someone else, that’s my cue to dig deeper into why I had that reaction.

text:
<@UG0EL18H3>'s advice about spending time with your feelings and trying to understand them before reacting reflexively is great in... (checks notes)... every other aspect of your life. It's the sort of thing that primary education ought to teach, but doesn't.

In terms of "the expectation is that criticism would bother you", it is normative to feel prickled by criticism, but the more productive framing is gratitude for an opportunity to improve this thing about which you care so much. There's nothing easy about moving from defensive to open hearted, but it's one of the most worthwhile things you can do.

text: The only surviving artifact from that project is a paper I delivered to the W3C in '02 or '03 about the TripleStore implementation. OTOH, my personal knowledge management system is basically a more advanced version of many of those ideas, and I already plan to do some show-and-tell about that when I can fence off enough time to do it properly.
